tldr: Vanilla ByT5-large trained on a meticulously constructed corpus built via a three-stage LLM sentence alignment pipeline, large-scale academic PDF mining, and strict orthographic normalization.
summary: The solution uses a vanilla google/byt5-large model with zero architectural modifications, treating data construction as the primary engineering challenge. A three-stage LLM pipeline generated clean sentence-level alignments from official files and corrupt metadata, while ~60 external academic PDFs were mined, OCR-processed, and translated to expand the corpus to over 100k pairs. Training utilized Adafactor with length-grouped batching, where setting β₁=0.9 stabilized gradients caused by non-uniform sequence lengths. The approach demonstrates that systematic data construction, text normalization, and multi-version extraction vastly outperform architectural tweaks or post-processing in low-resource NMT tasks.
extensive_summary:
## Core Approach & Data Pipeline
The solution is entirely data-centric, using a vanilla google/byt5-large model with zero architectural changes. The primary engineering effort focused on constructing a massive, clean training corpus via a three-stage LLM pipeline applied to non-overlapping official data sets:
- **Breaker**: Used OARE API word indices and physical line numbers to prompt an LLM to split full English translations into sentence-level chunks with precise word_range anchors. Yielded 9,378 pairs from 1,558 documents.
- **Fixer**: Re-anchored corrupt S_meta fragments by matching normalized word forms to the physical tablet structure, using existing fragments only as coarse hints. Yielded 11,302 pairs from 1,416 documents.
- **Generator**: Attempted pseudo-translation from morphological metadata alone; outputs were too noisy and hurt scores, so it was dropped.

## External Data & Corpus Expansion
The single largest score driver was mining ~60 independent academic PDFs (ArAn, Belleten, DTCF, ATDD, HAL, international journals, monographs) covering Old Assyrian cuneiform. These were OCR-processed and extracted into ~60,654 sentence pairs across 149 sources. Non-English sources (Turkish, German, French) were translated directly into English by the LLM pipeline, expanding Akkadian surface forms without multi-target issues. A Prefer-EN dedup strategy kept only the English version when the same tablet ID appeared across languages. To balance exposure, Turkish sources were amplified 3× via multi-version extraction, while fresh English academic papers were explicitly boosted 2×.

## Text Normalization & Chunking
Source-side text normalization unified highly variable cuneiform orthography using strict rules: sz / s, + consonant → š, , + consonant → ṭ, subscript 2 after vowel → acute accent (a2→á, etc.), subscript 3 after vowel → grave accent (a3→à, etc.), higher subscripts → plain ASCII digits, hamza variants → deleted, ḫ / Ḫ → h / H, and determinatives → {d}, {ki}. Consecutive sentences were concatenated into document-level examples capped at 768 bytes (matching ByT5-large's max_source_length), providing inter-sentence context and implicit augmentation via variable chunking boundaries.

## Training Configuration & Hyperparameters
Three training runs were conducted. The submitted Run 1 used google/byt5-large, LR 7e-5, Adafactor (β₁=None, decay_rate=-0.8, clip=1.0), effective batch size 128 (32×4), 16 epochs, 800 warmup steps, linear LR scheduler, weight decay 0.01, and max_grad_norm=10.0. group_by_length=True minimized padding waste but caused non-uniform gradient magnitudes due to similar sequence lengths per batch. This instability was resolved in later runs by setting β₁=0.9 in Adafactor, introducing momentum to dampen per-step variance. Run 3 achieved the highest public score (42.4) with ~100,143 pairs, 14 epochs, and β₁=0.9, but Run 1 was selected for submission due to better public/private alignment.

## Key Findings & Lessons
The competition was fundamentally a data bottleneck problem; the public/private LB gap proved that continuous external data collection outweighs architectural tuning. Post-processing name translations (PN/GN) failed because the test set's diverse sources use inconsistent conventions, making fuzzy-matching logic error-prone. Similarly, adding word-level dictionary translation pairs as training samples provided no benefit. The most impactful techniques were the LLM alignment pipeline, external PDF mining, orthographic normalization, and multi-version extraction for implicit augmentation.
preprocessing: Subscript 2 after vowel → acute accent (a2→á, e2→é, i2→í, u2→ú)
Subscript 3 after vowel → grave accent (a3→à, e3→è, i3→ì, u3→ù)
Prefer-EN dedup for duplicate tablet IDs across languages
LLM-based sentence alignment and corruption fixing
augmentations: Multi-version extraction (3 runs per non-English source) for implicit translation variance
Document-level augmentation: concatenating consecutive sentences into 768-byte chunks
2× boost for English academic papers to balance exposure with amplified Turkish sources
what_worked: English prioritization via Prefer-EN dedup (Small positive)
what_did_not_work: Translation Generator (LLM translations from morphological metadata alone hurt scores)
Word-level dictionary augmentation (adding translate pairs as training samples showed no benefit)
PN/GN post-processing (fuzzy-matching name translations failed due to test set diversity and inconsistent conventions)
critical_findings: Public/private LB gap indicated that collecting more external data would have improved the private score more than model tuning.
group_by_length=True caused non-uniform gradient magnitudes, resolved by setting β₁=0.9 in Adafactor to add momentum.
Test set comes from diverse sources with inconsistent name conventions, making PN/GN post-processing ineffective and error-prone.
